elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a method, \MethodName, for optimizing neural signed distance functions, based on a relation between the solution of a screened Poisson equation and the distance function.
elements.sent-2411-005: Existing losses such as the eikonal loss cannot guarantee the recovered implicit function to be a distance function, even when the implicit function satisfies the eikonal equation almost everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, the eikonal loss suffers from stability issues in optimization and the remedies that introduce area or divergence minimization can lead to oversmoothing.
elements.sent-2411-007: We address these challenges by designing a loss function that when minimized can converge to the true distance function, is stable, and naturally penalize large surface area.
elements.sent-2411-008: We provide theoretical analysis and experiments on both challenging 2D and 3D datasets and show that our method provide better surface reconstruction and more accurate distance approximation.
elements.sent-2411-009: Learning neural signed distance function requires designing loss functions so that the zero crossings of the implicit function is at the desired locations (e.g., adhere to a sparse input point cloud), and that the implicit function returns correct signed distance.
elements.sent-2411-010: The design of the loss proves to be challenging for shapes with complex topology and details (\Cref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: We propose \MethodName, a method based on a relation between screened Poisson equation and distance that enjoys both theoretical and practical benefits.
elements.sent-2411-012: A major challenge of signed distance function optimization is to ensure that the implicit function indeed outputs the signed distance.
elements.sent-2411-013: A standard regularization loss used is the eikonal equation: it constrains the norm of the gradient of an implicit function to be $1$ almost everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-014: If the implicit function is a signed distance function, then it satisfies the eikonal equation.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the converse is not true.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{figures/1d_improved.pdf} \caption{ We illustrate an 1D neural signed distance function optimization using the classical eikonal loss~\cite{gropp2020implicit} and our model. The $x$ axis is the domain and the $y$ axis shows the output of the impilcit function. The gray dashed line is the reference signed distance function. The middle and bottom rows show the intermediate and final states of the optimization respectively. The eikonal loss is incapable of converging to the actual signed distance function (dashed line), even when the function satisfies the eikonal equation almost everywhere. Our method converges to the ground truth by modeling a screened Poisson equation. } \label{fig:1d}
elements.sent-2411-016: Another key challenge lies in the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is known that the eikonal equation regularization is unstable~\cite{yang2023steik , and the instability can make optimization converge to suboptimal results or even diverge.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, to combat both the ill-posedness and the instability of the optimization, existing methods need to penalize the surface area of the shape, which leads to a delicate balance between over- and under-smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-019: We propose a simple model that can be used together with the eikonal equation to address all the challenges above.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our model, \MethodName, is based on a screened Poisson equation and a classical relation between heat transfer and distance~\cite{Varadhan:1967:BFS , that was popular for approximating both Euclidean and geodesic distance~\cite{Crane:2013:GHN,Belyaev:2020:ASD .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Using this relation, we design a loss function for optimizing neural signed distance functions.
elements.sent-2411-022: As our contributions, we show, both theoretically and empirically, that our model.
elements.sent-2411-023: Modeling and reconstructing surfaces using implicit functions has a long history in computer vision and graphics~\cite{Blinn:1982:GAS,Bloomenthal:1990:ITI,Hoppe:1992:SRU,Curless:1996:VMB,Izadi:2011:KR3 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Recently, there is a surge of interests to model surfaces using neural implicit functions~\cite{mescheder2019occupancy,chen2019learning,Davies:2020:EWN,Williams:2021:NSF,Xie:2022:NFV .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Neural implicit representations are favored for their compactness, ability to represent highly-detailed surfaces, and compatibility to gradient-based optimization~\cite{Mehta:2022:LST .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Among the classes of implicit surfaces, signed distance functions, where the implicit function outputs the distance to the closest point on the surface, and the sign represents whether the point is inside or outside the solid object, is one of the most popular variants.
elements.sent-2411-028: Signed distance functions enable an efficient rendering algorithm called sphere tracing~\cite{Hart:1996:STG,Seyb:2019:NST,Galin:2020:STL,Sharp:2022:SDG and helps geometry compression~\cite{Takikawa:2021:NGL .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: They can also be used for collision detection~\cite{Fuhrmann:2003:DFR,Guendelman:2003:NRB,Macklin:2020:LOR , and numerical simulation~\cite{Sawhney:2020:MCG .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Traditionally, signed distance functions are often represented as grids~\cite{pumarola2022visco .}
elements.sent-2411-031: More recently, neural signed distance functions have become an appealing geometric representation~\cite{Park:2019:DLC,sitzmann2020implicit,Coiffier:2024:1ND .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Regularizing and initializing neural signed distance functions.
elements.sent-2411-033: A commonly used loss for optimizing neural signed distance functions is the eikonal regularization, which encourages the norm of the gradient of the implicit function to be one~\cite{gropp2020implicit,Yariv:2020:MNS .}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, the eikonal regularization alone is far from sufficient, as 1) it can contain many undesirable local minima~\cite{yang2023steik , and 2) minimizing eikonal loss does not necessarily lead to a distance field~\cite{marschner2023constructive,pumarola2022visco .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Previous works have proposed initialization to bootstrap the optimization with a simple shape~\cite{gropp2020implicit,ben2022digs .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Recent works show that minimizing the divergence and the surface area of the implicit function can lead to better reconstruction~\cite{ben2022digs,park2024p,Zhang:2022:CRN and stabilizes the optimization~\cite{yang2023steik .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Second-order information is also often used~\cite{Zhang:2022:CRN,Wang:2024:AGH for regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Specialized architectures have been proposed for limiting the Lipchitz constant of the implicit function~\cite{Coiffier:2024:1ND and for representing high-frequency details~\cite{sitzmann2020implicit,Takikawa:2021:NGL,Li:2023:NHN .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Lipman~\cite{Lipman:2021:PTD showed a connection between the regularization of occupancy function optimization and an approximated signed distance field using the phase transition theory of fluids.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We propose a new loss and method for optimizing signed distance functions.
elements.sent-2411-041: While derived from very different mathematics, our loss turns out to have a close connection to Lipman's PHASE loss.
elements.sent-2411-042: We show that our theory leads to significantly more numerically stable optimization, and a different conclusion in the parameter setting that leads to drastically different results.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our derivation is inspired by a relation between heat transfer and approximated distance~\cite{Varadhan:1967:BFS .}
elements.sent-2411-045: This relation has been used in computer graphics for efficiently computing the geodesic distance on surfaces~\cite{Crane:2013:GHN,Belyaev:2020:ASD,Sharp:2019:VHM,Feng:2024:HMG .}
elements.sent-2411-046: We apply the relation for optimizing neural signed distance functions, and analyze the properties of the resulting optimization in this context.
elements.sent-2411-047: Given an input point cloud $\bm{x _i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ (we focus on 2D and 3D in this work) with $N$ points without normal information that lies on an unknown surface $\Gamma$ of a solid object $S$, our goal is to find a signed distance function $f(\bm{x )$, such that \begin{equation}f(\bm{x}) = \begin{cases} -d_{\Gamma}(\bm{x}) \text{ if } \bm{x} \in S d_{\Gamma}(\bm{x}) \text{ otherwise, } \end{cases}\end{equation} where $d_{\Gamma (\bm{x )$ denotes the closest distance from point $\bm{x}$ to the surface $\Gamma$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: In practice, we approximate the signed distance function $f(\bm{x )$ using a neural network $u(\bm{x ): \mathbb{R ^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ parameterized by its weights and use gradient-based optimization to figure out the weights.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Previous work (e.g.,~\cite{gropp2020implicit ) often uses a linear combination of a boundary loss $L_{\text{boundary }$ and a eikonal loss $L_{\text{eikonal }$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:boundary_loss} L_{boundary} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left| u(x_i) \right|^{p}, \label{eq:eikonal_loss} L_{eikonal} = \int_{\Omega} \left| \left\| \nabla u(x) \right\| - 1 \right|^p \, dx, \quad p = 1 \, or \, 2,\end{equation} where $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R ^d$ is our domain of interest.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-054: We will be using a key relation between the screen Poisson equation and the distance.
elements.sent-2411-055: We first define a screened Poisson equation over a heat field $h(\bm{x ) : \mathbb{R ^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, with an absorption coefficient $\lambda$, and set a Dirichlet boundary condition to be $1$ on our target surface $\Gamma$: \begin{equation}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \nabla^2 h(\bm{x}) - \lambda^2 h(\bm{x}) = 0 \quad \forall \bm{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \setminus \Gamma \lim_{||\bm{x}|| \to\infty} h(\bm{x}) = 0, \quad h(\bm{x}) = 1 \quad \forall \bm{x} \in \Gamma. \end{aligned} \right. \label{eq:heat}\end{equation} If a heat field $h_{\lambda}$ is a solution to the partial differential equation above, then the following equation holds~\cite{Varadhan:1967:BFS : \begin{equation}\lim_{\lambda \to\infty} \frac{1}{\lambda}\ln(h_{\lambda}(\bm{x})) = -d_{\Gamma}(\bm{x}). \label{eq:heat_to_distance}\end{equation} \Cref{fig:differentlambdas shows the intuition.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-314: While derived from very different mathematical principles and a different motivation, our method turns out to have a close relation to the PHASE model proposed by Lipman.
elements.sent-2411-315: The PHASE model essentially simulates two fluids finding equilibrium in a container by minimizing an energy functional.
elements.sent-2411-316: A smooth approximation of the indicator of different fluids is involved and interpreted as an occupancy function $o(\bm{x )$ such that $o$ outputs $1$ when outside of the object, $-1$ when inside the object, and $0$ when exactly on the surface.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Lipman further adds a reconstruction loss to encourage $o(\bm{x )$ to vanish at the boundary and adapts the Van der Waals-Cahn-Hilliard theory of phase transitions~\cite{modica1987gradient, sternberg1988effect , resulting in the following functional to minimize: \begin{equation}\mathcal{F} (o) = w_b \mathcal{L}(o) + \int_\Omega \epsilon \| \nabla o \|^2 + W(o), \label{eq:phase_log_transform}\end{equation} where $w_b$ is the weight of the reconstruction loss, $\mathcal{L (o)$ is the reconstruction loss that encourages $o$ to be zero at the boundary, $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant, and $W(o)$ is a potential.}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: Lipman showed that by choosing a double-well potential $W(o) = o^2 - 2|o| + 1$, the minimizer can be converted into an approximated signed distance function $s$ through a log transform: \begin{equation}s = -\sqrt{\epsilon} \ln\left(1 - \left|o\right|\right) \text{sign}(o). \label{eq:phase}\end{equation} It turns out that our heat field $h$ when optimized under our heat loss is closely related to the regularized occupancy function $o$.
elements.sent-2411-319: If we set $h = 1 - |o|$ and $\lambda = \epsilon^{-\frac{1 {2 }$ and solve for the screened Poisson equation (\Cref{eq:heat ), we would obtain PHASE's regularized occupancy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: However, there are several crucial differences between our approach and PHASE where our theory and derivation has led to additional insights and huge differences in implementations and results.
elements.sent-2411-321: First, based on a theoretical framework aiming the area minimization, PHASE's theory promotes setting the weight $w_b$ of the boundary loss (\Cref{eq:boundary_loss ) to $w_b = \epsilon^{\alpha}$ where $\alpha \in (\frac{1 {4 , \frac{1 {2 )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: However, we found that finding the exact minimal area is detrimental to the optimization, as discussed in \Cref{sec:bg .}
elements.sent-2411-323: On the contrary, according to our theory and experiments, we find it necessary to maintain boundaries between points and set the weight $w_b$ to a much higher value to ensure the boundary condition of the screened Poisson equation is satisfied (\Cref{eq:heat ), as states in main text.}
elements.sent-2411-324: Theorem 2 in the PHASE paper states that to achieve minimal area, $w_b$ should converge to $0$ as $\epsilon$ approaches an infinitesimal value.
elements.sent-2411-325: However, this interpretation does not align with the nature of the signed distance function reconstruction task which should complete the manifold and connect points, as discussed in \Cref{sec:bg , and may lead to unintended consequences as follows: When the boundary weight $w_b$ is overly small, the signed distance function values of boundary points cannot even converge to close to 0.}
elements.sent-2411-326: When the weight $w_b$ is still not large enough, their output collapses from a surface-based-distance signed distance function to a point-cloud-based-distance unsigned distance function upon reaching the target where the boundaries are only point clouds and the area becomes almost zero.
elements.sent-2411-327: In contrast, our model demonstrates robust and accurate performance, effectively connecting points and interpolating boundaries by taking advantages of neural network's spectral bias~\cite{Rahaman:2019:SBN , with a large boundary weight $w_b$ and an adaptive absorption $\lambda$, as discussed in \Cref{sec:disc .}}
elements.sent-2411-328: In addition to \Cref{fig:phase-boundary-weight , we show more empirical results on our 2D dataset supporting our claim here in the supplementary.}
elements.sent-2411-329: In \Cref{fig:phase-boundary-weight-circle , we show the visual results of PHASE on a single circle across different boundary weight choices, and compute the surface reconstruction and distance metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-330: We show visual results on the rest of the 2D dataset in \Cref{fig:phase-boundary-weight-more , and the mean metrics for each boundary loss weight over the whole 2D dataset in \Cref{tab:phase-boundary-weight-metrics .}}
elements.sent-2411-331: In these experiments, we use eikonal loss weight $0.1$, as provided in the PHASE paper.
elements.sent-2411-332: We also show one example from ShapeNet in \Cref{fig:phase-boundary-weight-3d , where we use eikonal loss weight $1.0$.}
elements.sent-2411-333: From our theory and the examples above, one can clearly see that using a small boundary loss weight is not the best strategy.

elements.sent-2411-343: We propose a method, \MethodName, for optimizing neural signed distance functions.
elements.sent-2411-344: Existing losses, such as the eikonal loss, act as necessary but insufficient constraints and cannot guarantee that the recovered implicit function represents a true distance function, even if the output minimizes these losses almost everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-345: Furthermore, the eikonal loss suffers from stability issues in optimization.
elements.sent-2411-346: Finally, in conventional methods, regularization losses that penalize surface area distort the reconstructed signed distance function.
elements.sent-2411-347: We address these challenges by designing a loss function using the solution of a screened Poisson equation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our loss, when minimized, provides an asymptotically sufficient condition to ensure the output converges to a true distance function.
elements.sent-2411-349: Our loss also leads to stable optimization and naturally penalizes large surface areas.
elements.sent-2411-350: We present theoretical analysis and experiments on both challenging 2D and 3D datasets and show that our method provides better surface reconstruction and a more accurate distance approximation.
elements.sent-2411-351: Learning neural signed distance function requires designing loss functions so that the zero crossings of the implicit function are at the desired locations (e.g., adhere to a sparse input point cloud), and that the implicit function returns the correct signed distance.
elements.sent-2411-352: Designing the loss function proves to be challenging for shapes with complex topology and details (\Cref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-353: We propose \MethodName, a method based on a relation between the screened Poisson equation and distance that enjoys both theoretical and practical benefits.
elements.sent-2411-354: A major challenge in signed distance function optimization is ensuring the implicit function follows the true distance.
elements.sent-2411-355: A common regularization loss used is the eikonal equation, which constrains the gradient norm of an implicit function to be $1$ almost everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-356: That is, satisfying the eikonal equation is only a necessary condition for the signed distance function, not a sufficient condition~\cite{marschner2023constructive .}
elements.sent-2411-357: Furthermore, to address optimization ill-posedness and instability, existing methods penalize the surface area of the shape.
elements.sent-2411-358: We prove this degrades the reconstruction quality and distorts the output level sets, thereby leading to a delicate balance between over- and under-smoothing.
elements.sent-2411-359: We propose a simple model that can address all the challenges above.
elements.sent-2411-360: Our model, \MethodName, is based on a screened Poisson equation and a classical relation between heat transfer and distance~\cite{Varadhan:1967:BFS that was popular for approximating both Euclidean and geodesic distance~\cite{Crane:2013:GHN,belyaev2015variational,Belyaev:2020:ASD .}}
